era_context: The year 1338 falls within the early phase of the Hundred Years' War, which had begun in 1337. The Yuan Dynasty in China was experiencing administrative decline and environmental stress. The Ottoman Empire was consolidating power in Anatolia. The Black Death was still confined to Central Asia. The Avignon Papacy continued to exercise spiritual authority from southern France. This was a period of significant political and military tension across Europe, Asia, and the Mediterranean.

events.title: English naval operations in the English Channel; Hundred Years' War escalates
events.description: In 1338, English forces under Edward III conducted naval operations in the English Channel and along the Flemish coast, attempting to establish control over sea lanes and support English allies in Flanders. These operations were part of the broader strategy to isolate France and establish English dominance in northern Europe. The naval campaigns of 1338 set the stage for the Battle of Sluys in 1340 and demonstrated the importance of naval power in medieval warfare.
events.certainty_note: English naval operations in 1338 are well-documented in contemporary English and French royal records.

events.title: Clement VI becomes Bishop of Arras; ecclesiastical advancement
events.description: Pierre Roger (the future Pope Clement VI) became Bishop of Arras in 1338, a position that enhanced his ecclesiastical status and political influence. This appointment reflected his growing prominence in the French church and his connections to the French royal court. His advancement through ecclesiastical ranks would culminate in his election as Pope in 1342.
key_figures: Pierre Roger / Bishop of Arras (future Pope Clement VI)
events.certainty_note: Pierre Roger's appointment as Bishop of Arras in 1338 is well-documented in ecclesiastical records.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty faces widespread drought and famine
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty experienced widespread drought and famine in 1338, affecting multiple provinces and causing significant agricultural disruption. These environmental crises were exacerbated by administrative corruption and the breakdown of irrigation systems. The famines of 1338 contributed to social unrest and would be cited by later historians as evidence of the Yuan Dynasty's loss of the Mandate of Heaven.
key_figures: Toghon Temür (Huizong Emperor, Yuan Dynasty, r. 1333–1368)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Dynasty records, compiled after 1368)

events.certainty_note: Drought and famine in 1338 are attested in Yuan records, but specific regional extent and casualty figures are difficult to verify. Later Ming historians may have emphasized Yuan environmental crises to justify their own dynasty's legitimacy.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia; consolidation of northwestern territories
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Orhan I continued its territorial consolidation in northwestern Anatolia in 1338, establishing administrative structures and military garrisons. Ottoman forces were systematically reducing rival Turkmen beyliks and expanding their control over former Byzantine territories. This expansion was facilitated by Byzantine weakness and the fragmentation of Anatolian political authority.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is well-attested in Byzantine sources, but Ottoman chronicles are less reliable for specific dates and details. 1338 is a conventional date for ongoing consolidation, but the exact timeline is uncertain.

events.title: Petrarch in Avignon; engagement with papal court
events.description: Francesco Petrarch was active in Avignon in 1338, engaging with the papal court and developing his intellectual network. Petrarch's presence in Avignon connected him to the centers of ecclesiastical and political power in Europe. His time in Avignon contributed to his development as a humanist scholar and his later prominence in European intellectual circles.
events.certainty_note: Petrarch's presence in Avignon in 1338 is confirmed by his own letters and contemporary accounts.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of English naval operations in 1338 is sometimes overstated; while they were militarily important, they did not lead to sustained English control of the Channel or immediate strategic advantage. The Battle of Sluys in 1340 would be a more decisive naval engagement. The Yuan Dynasty's environmental crises in 1338 are documented, but the causal links to later rebellion are inferred rather than directly attested. Multiple factors contributed to Yuan decline, and environmental stress alone was not determinative.
historiographic_note: The year 1338 is moderately well-documented for European events, with contemporary chronicles from England, France, and the Papacy providing detailed accounts. Asian sources (Chinese, Ottoman) are more fragmentary and often compiled centuries after the events. The surviving sources reflect elite perspectives (royal courts, papal bureaucracy); peasant and non-elite experiences are largely invisible. Ottoman sources are particularly problematic, as they were compiled in the 15th–16th centuries and may reflect later political narratives. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific regions are absent from surviving records.

graph_edges.note: English naval operations in 1338 led to Battle of Sluys in 1340
Yuan drought and famine in 1338 continued in 1340, contributing to social unrest
Petrarch's engagement with Avignon court in 1338 contributed to his later coronation as Poet Laureate in 1341
